does: This paper introduces DILA, a mechanistic interpretability module that transforms uninterpretable dense embeddings into sparse embeddings where each nonzero element represents a learned medical concept. The method disentangles complex multilabel medical coding predictions by discovering thousands of globally interpretable features through an automated pipeline that uses large language models to examine token activations for each dictionary feature. The authors demonstrate through human evaluation that their sparse embeddings achieve at least 50 percent improvement in human understandability compared to dense embeddings, and represent label-to-concept relationships through a sparse interpretable matrix that reveals the mechanistic structure underlying predictions.
connects: This work addresses the longstanding challenge of interpretability in extreme multilabel classification, building on recent mechanistic interpretability research that aims to decompose neural network representations into human-understandable components. It extends prior work on sparse feature discovery and dictionary learning approaches by applying them specifically to high-dimensional medical coding tasks, where interpretability is critical for clinical adoption. The paper combines techniques from local interpretability methods with global mechanistic approaches, responding to limitations of existing explanation methods that fail to provide comprehensive insight into overall prediction mechanisms. It leverages recent advances in LLM-based concept discovery while addressing the specific constraints of medical coding prediction at scale.
enables: This work opens pathways toward fully interpretable medical AI systems where predictions can be understood in terms of explicit learned medical concepts rather than opaque embeddings, potentially facilitating clinical validation and regulatory approval of AI coding systems. The sparse feature discovery pipeline could extend to other high-dimensional multilabel domains requiring interpretability, from legal document classification to scientific article tagging. The method enables future work on mechanistic understanding of how neural networks capture domain-specific knowledge, post-hoc concept editing for correcting model behaviors, and automated medical ontology discovery. It also points toward human-in-the-loop systems where clinicians can directly interact with and verify the learned medical concepts driving predictions.
